response.evidence: The data shows systematic disparities in acceptance rates across racial groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), followed by White applicants (79.2-84.5%), while Black or African American applicants have substantially lower acceptance rates (64.8-73.0%), and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants show similarly lower rates (64.8-75.3%). These differences persist despite comparable average debt-to-income ratios (43-44% across all groups) and similar loan-to-value ratios. The 15-23 percentage point gap in acceptance rates between Asian and Black applicants, given similar financial metrics, suggests differential treatment based on race.

The data shows systematic disparities in acceptance rates across racial groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), followed by White applicants (79.2-84.5%), while Black or African American applicants have substantially lower acceptance rates (64.8-73.0%), and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants show similarly lower rates (64.8-75.3%). These disparities of 15-23 percentage points persist despite comparable average debt-to-income ratios (43-44% across all groups) and loan-to-value ratios. For example, Asian females (non-Hispanic) have 86.5% acceptance with average income of $176k, while Black females (non-Hispanic) have 73.0% acceptance with comparable debt ratios but lower average income of $136k. However, White females (non-Hispanic) with similar income ($143k) achieve 83.8% acceptance. The pattern of lower acceptance rates for certain racial minorities, particularly Black applicants, even when financial metrics are comparable, suggests racial disparities in loan approval outcomes.

The data shows substantial disparities in acceptance rates across racial groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), followed by White applicants (79.2-84.5%), while Black or African American applicants have notably lower acceptance rates (64.8-73.0%), and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants range from 64.8-75.3%. These differences persist despite similar average debt-to-income ratios (43-44% across all groups) and comparable loan-to-value ratios. For example, Asian females (non-Hispanic) have an 86.5% acceptance rate with avg income of 177K, while Black females (non-Hispanic) have a 73.0% acceptance rate with similar debt-to-income ratio (43.9% vs 43.7%) but lower avg income of 136K. The consistent pattern of lower acceptance rates for certain racial groups, even when financial metrics are comparable, suggests potential discrimination in the loan approval process.

The data shows systematic disparities in acceptance rates across racial groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), followed by White applicants (75.9-84.5%), while Black or African American applicants have notably lower acceptance rates (64.8-73.0%), and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants range from 64.8-75.3%. These differences persist even when comparing groups with similar average loan amounts, debt-to-income ratios (all groups cluster around 43-44%), and property values. For example, Black or African American males have an average loan amount of $429K and acceptance rate of 70.5%, while White males with similar demographics have $478K average loans but 84.5% acceptance rate. The consistency of these patterns across sex and ethnicity subgroups suggests systematic disparities that warrant investigation for potential discrimination.

These differences persist even though financial metrics (avg_debt_to_income_ratio: 43-44 across all groups, avg_loan_to_value_ratio: 54-71 across groups) are comparable across racial categories. The 15-23 percentage point gap in acceptance rates between Asian and Black applicants, despite similar financial qualifications, suggests racial disparities in the loan approval process.
